title: Extra-Wide Triple-Pedestal Farmhouse Table
summary: A nearly 8-foot farmhouse table on three boxed 2x4 pedestals with angled decorative cross supports, wide enough to need the third leg for real support.
description:
At close to 96" long, this table is wide enough that a standard two-pedestal base would sag in the middle under real load — the third, center pedestal is what makes a table this size structurally honest rather than just visually balanced. Build all three pedestals identically and check that a 2x6 slides cleanly into each pedestal's opening before you move on; a tight or loose fit on even one pedestal throws off the beam alignment down the line.

The angled cross braces on each pedestal are cut at 22-1/2° — an eighth-turn angle, not a standard 45° — so double-check your miter saw's angle setting against the cut list before running through all 24 of those pieces. The beams that run the length of the table get slid onto the assembled pedestals rather than boxed in around them, which is what lets this design go together and come apart again for moving a table this size through doorways.

tools.note: Double-check the 22-1/2° angle setting before cutting the 24 cross-support pieces.

materials.name: Pine project panels, 16" x 96"
materials.note: Or 1x lumber for the tabletop, at least 47-1/2" x 95-3/4" total.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 22-1/2° off square, long-point to long-point.
Both ends cut at 22-1/2° off square, long-point to long-point.
One end cut at 22-1/2° off square, longest-point measurement; other end square.
Both ends cut at 22-1/2° off square, long-point to long-point.

steps.title: Build the six main leg pieces
steps.body: Build six main leg pieces (two per pedestal) with glue and 2" brad nails, keeping all outside edges flush. Confirm a 2x6 can slide into each opening as you build — this is what the beams will thread through later, so a tight or loose fit here is much easier to fix now than after the pedestals are finished. Build this for all three legs.

steps.title: Add the first outer 2x4 layer
steps.body: Use 2-1/2" self-tapping screws to attach the first layer of outer 2x4 boards to the top and bottom of each leg assembly. Repeat for all three pedestal legs.

steps.title: Add the angled cross supports
steps.body: Attach the decorative cross support pieces with 2" nails and glue — double-check your miter saw is actually set to 22-1/2°, not the more common 45°, before running through all 24 pieces.

steps.title: Attach the remaining bottom piece
steps.body: Attach the remaining bottom cap piece to each leg assembly with 2" nails and glue.

steps.title: Slide the legs onto the beams
steps.body: Mark the beam locations for all three pedestals and slide the finished legs onto the beams. Attach with 2" nails or screws.

steps.title: Build the tabletop frame and attach the top
steps.body: Build the tabletop frame using pocket holes, then attach your chosen tabletop material to the frame with 2" screws and glue.

steps.title: Attach the base to the tabletop
steps.body: Center the three-pedestal base under the tabletop frame and attach with 2-1/2" screws.

images.alt: Extra-wide farmhouse dining table on three boxed pedestal legs with angled cross supports
